Patch Set 1:

> I see what you are saying. Its not very intuitive. In my stats code
 > I did:
 > 
 > /* parse_stats will be called when netlink sends replys */
 > sock = llapi_netlink_register(&parse_stats, &paths, false);
 > ...
 > rc = llapi_netlink_bind_group(sock, LUSTRE_STATS_GENL_NAME, group);
 > ...
 > /* need to request stats but HSM doesn't need to do this step */
 > rc = llapi_netlink_send_msg(sock, LUSTRE_STATS_GENL_NAME, 0,
 > LUSTRE_STATS_CMD_QUERY,
 > LUSTRE_STATS_VERSION, parse_query,
 > &paths);
 > ...
 > rc = nl_recvmsgs_default(sock);
 > ...
 > llapi_netlink_unregister(sock);
 > 
 > Suggestions to make this better?

No, it's fine now that I see it.  A layer with an examples of bi-directional communication would help immensely.  But that's not what this is.

Note that it has been requested that instead of direct netlink callbacks we create a generic internal call back for netlink that presents that data in struct cYAML pieces. I will be looking into making that work but it will take some time.

Patch Set 14: Code-Review-1

(18 comments)

IMHO lustre_netlink_{send,recv}_msg() are unnecessary wrappers around libnl's API. But since this is not part of the public API of Lustre, we can try them out for a while and decide later whether or not they should be removed/improved/left as is.

Patch Set 14:

(2 comments)

Quentin after some discuss with the LNet people they want netlink to be hidden inside of lnetconfig. What they like to see is cYAML data being used to send and receive messages. I doing some local testing with this new approach to see how it works out.

commit.subject: LU-9680 utils: add netlink infrastructure
commit.message:
LU-9680 utils: add netlink infrastructure

Netlink was designed as a successor to ioctl as defined under
RFC 3549. There are several advantages to using netlink over
ioctls or virtual file system interfaces like proc. Collecting
proc doesn't scale well which was seen with power drain on Android
phones. A netlink implementation was developed to remove this
performance hit. Details can be read at:

https://lwn.net/Articles/406975

Besides the scaling gains the other benefit is the flexiblity
with API changes. Adding or removing information to be transmitted
doesn't require creating a new interface like ioctl do. Instead
you add new code to handle the stream of attributes read from the
socket. Lastly you can multiplex data to N listeners with groups
using one request.

This patch adds netlink handling in a generic way that can be
used by the libyaml library. This greatly lowers the barrier by
only requiring the implementor to understand the libyaml API.
